title: What is Visual Anomaly Detection?
concept: Anomaly detection identifies images or regions that deviate significantly from what's 'normal', without needing labeled examples of every possible way something can go wrong.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the core tradeoff Approximate Nearest Neighbor search makes?

options.text: A small chance of missing the exact match, for massive speed gains at scale
Perfect accuracy, but only for small databases
No speed benefit at all compared to brute-force search

exercises.explain: Yes! ANN trades a tiny accuracy risk for dramatic speed at scale.

cards.title: You know it when you see it — but can't list every case
cards.body: A factory inspector can instantly spot a weirdly cracked bottle on the line, even if they've never seen that EXACT crack pattern before. How do you train a model to recognize 'weird' without showing it every possible way something can go wrong?

cards.title: Visual Anomaly Detection
cards.body: Anomaly detection identifies images or image regions that deviate significantly from a learned notion of 'normal', flagging them as unusual — without requiring labeled examples of every specific way an anomaly can occur.

cards.title: Spotting the one out-of-tune violin
cards.body: In a full orchestra playing in perfect harmony, an out-of-tune violin stands out immediately to a trained ear — not because the listener memorized every possible wrong note, but because they deeply know what 'in tune' sounds like, and anything else jumps out.

cards.title: Bottle defect detection
cards.body: A factory anomaly detector is trained ONLY on photos of correctly-formed bottles. When a bottle with a rare, never-before-seen crack pattern passes the camera, the model flags it as anomalous simply because it doesn't match the 'normal bottle' pattern it learned — no crack-specific training needed.

cards.title: It's not the same as standard classification
cards.body: It's a common mix-up to treat anomaly detection like classification with a 'defective' class. But defects are often extremely rare and infinitely varied (a crack, a scratch, a dent, a discoloration...) — training a classifier to recognize every specific defect type is impractical. Anomaly detection instead just learns what 'normal' looks like.

cards.title: Classification vs. Anomaly Detection
cards.body: Two different strategies for spotting problems:

left.text:
Needs labeled examples of EVERY defect type
Struggles with rare, novel defects
Fixed set of known problems

right.text:
Only needs 'normal' examples to train
Flags anything unusual, even novel defects
No fixed defect catalog needed

exercises.prompt: What is the core idea behind visual anomaly detection?

options.text: Learning what 'normal' looks like, then flagging anything that deviates significantly from it
Memorizing every possible specific defect type in advance
Classifying images into a fixed set of 100 predefined categories
Compressing images to detect file corruption

exercises.explain: Correct! Anomaly detection learns normality and flags deviations from it.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Anomaly detection requires labeled training examples of every specific type of defect.
exercises.explain: False. Anomaly detection typically only needs examples of 'normal', flagging anything that deviates from that pattern.

exercises.sentence: Anomaly detection flags images that ___ significantly from a learned notion of normal.
exercises.explain: Anomaly detection is fundamentally about measuring deviation from normality.

exercises.prompt: Why is anomaly detection often preferred over standard classification for factory defect inspection?

options.text: Defects are rare and infinitely varied, making it impractical to collect labeled examples of every possible defect type
Classification always outperforms anomaly detection for any defect task
Anomaly detection cannot process factory images at all

exercises.explain: Correct! Anomaly detection's normal-only training sidesteps the rare-defect-labeling problem.

exercises.prompt: A pharmaceutical company wants to detect defective pills on a production line, but new, never-before-seen defect types occasionally appear. Which approach handles novel, unseen defect types better?

options.text: Anomaly detection, since it flags anything deviating from the learned 'normal pill' pattern, including entirely new defect types
A fixed classifier trained only on previously seen defect categories, since it never needs updating
Neither approach can ever detect a defect type it hasn't seen before
Manual inspection is always superior and should replace all vision-based approaches

exercises.explain: Exactly! Anomaly detection's strength is flagging novel deviations without needing prior examples. Next: how do you actually train a model using ONLY normal examples?
